name: Espagnol
tip: Ñ is unique to Spanish. Spanish uses de and del.

name: Portugais
tip: Portuguese includes the letters Ç, Ã, Õ, Â, and Ê. Portuguese uses da, do, das and dos.

name: Italien
tip: Italian is the most spoken language in Italy. It uses many double consonants, and typically has a very large number of i’s. You can also find ì, é and à. Most words will end with a vowel.

name: Allemand
tip: The German language uses the special letters Ä, Ö, Ü and ß.

name: Néerlandais
tip: Dutch is a Germanic language. It features a lot of double vowels. Typical letter clusters are ij, ieuw and oe. “IJ“ written as one capitalised letter is also unique to the language.

name: Norvégien
tip: The Norwegian language contains the special letters Æ, Ø and Å.

name: Suédois
tip: The Swedish alphabet contains the special letters Å, Ä and Ö.

name: Danois
tip: The Danish alphabet contains these special letters: Æ, Ø, and Å.

name: Finnois
tip: It has a lot of long words and double vowels. The Finnish alphabet includes the letters ä and ö.

name: Islandais
tip: The Icelandic language contains a couple of unique letters. These are some of the most recognizable ones: ð þ and æ. Vowels can have the following accents: á é í ó ö ú ý. The uppercase version of ð is Ð.

name: Letton
tip: Within the Baltic languages, the following letters are unique to Latvian: Vowels with a macron (horizontal stripe): Ā Ē Ī, consonants with a small hook: Ģ Ķ Ļ Ņ.

name: Lituanien
tip: Within the Baltic languages, the following letters are unique to Lithuanian: Vowels with small hooks: Ą Ę Į Ų, Ė with a single dot.

name: Estonien
tip: Within the Baltic languages, the following letters are unique to Lithuanian: Vowels with umlauts (two dots): Ä Ö Ü, Õ with a tilde.

name: Russe
tip: The Russian language contains the special letters Ы, Э, Ё (also used in Mongolian) and Ъ (also used in Bulgarian).

name: Ukrainien
tip: These letters are unique to Ukrainian, compared to Russian: Є є (not to be confused with Э э in Russian and Belarusian.), Ґ ґ, І і, Ї ї.

name: Bulgare
tip: Bulgaria mainly uses the Cyrillic alphabet. However, a lot of signs will also have the Latin alphabet. The letter “Ъ” is only found in Bulgarian and Russian, although it is fairly uncommon in Russian.

name: Serbe
tip: These letters are unique to Serbian: Ђ ђ Ћ ћ. These letters are found in Serbian and Macedonian: Љ љ Њ њ Џ џ.

name: Macédonien
tip: The letters ‘Ѓ’ and ‘Ќ’ are unique to Macedonian. ‘Љ’, ‘Њ’, and ‘Џ’ are only in Serbian and Macedonian.

name: Mongol
tip: Mongolian Cyrillic contains the letters Ɵ and Ү (also exist in the Kyrgyz and Kazakh languages), which do not exist in the Russian alphabet. Additionally, the use of double vowels, such as aa, ƟƟ or ЭЭ, is common in Mongolian.

name: Kazakh
tip: Kazakhstan uses both Cyrillic and Latin alphabets. The following letters are found in the Kazakh but not Russian alphabet: Ә (Ä), Ғ (Ǵ), Қ (Q), Ң (Ń), Ө (Ö), Ұ (W), Ү (Ü), Һ (H), І (I). Kazakh is related to Turkish, so the Latin text can appear similar.

name: Kirghiz
tip: The Cyrillic script used for Kyrgyz includes additional characters not found in Russian Cyrillic, such as: Ө Ү Ң.

name: Polonais
tip: The Polish alphabet includes: Ł Ż Ń Ć Ó (unique to Polish); Ą Ę (also found in Lithuanian); Ś, Ź (also found in Montenegrin). Polish does not use V, instead, it uses W a lot, the only Slavic language to do so. Other unusually common consonants are the Z, K, and C.

name: Tchèque
tip: Czech uses the letters Ř, Ě and Ů;

name: Slovaque
tip: Slovak uses the letters Ľ, Ô, Ä, Ŕ and Ĺ.

name: Croate
tip: ć et đ sont uniques au croate latin. Attention : č et ć sont deux lettres distinctes !

name: Slovène
tip: č, š, ž seulement, le plus sobre des slaves latins.

name: Roumain
tip: Romanian is a Romance language similar to Italian, containing the following special letters: Ă ă Ș ș Ț ț Î î.

name: Hongrois
tip: The Hungarian language has a unique look, with a very large number of acute accents, umlauts over o’s, and long words. The letters Ő and Ű, with double acute accents, are unique to the language.

name: Albanais
tip: Words often contain the letter Ë, ë, and sometimes Ç ç.

name: Maltais
tip: Maltese uses the Latin script, but with unique letters, which include: Ċ ċ Ġ ġ Ħ ħ Ż ż.

name: Turc
tip: The Turkish language has a fairly unique look. It uses the following special characters: Ç ç and Ş ş with a cedilla; Ö ö and Ü ü with an umlaut ; Ğ ğ with a breve; otless I ı; Capital İ with a dot.

name: Grec
tip: Alphabet grec — repère α, β, γ, δ, ω, π, σ, λ, θ, φ

name: Arabe
tip: Écriture cursive de droite à gauche, points sous/sur les lettres — pas de majuscules

name: Hébreu
tip: Lettres carrées de droite à gauche — moins cursif que l'arabe, peu de points diacritiques visibles

name: Chinois
tip: Caractères complexes (hanzi) seuls — pas de hiragana/katakana (japonais) ni de hangul (coréen)

name: Japonais
tip: Mélange de hiragana (courbé, ひらがな), katakana (anguleux, カタカナ) et kanji — toujours les trois ensemble

name: Coréen
tip: Blocs carrés avec cercles et lignes (hangul) — 한글 très reconnaissable, aucun caractère commun avec le chinois

name: Hindi
tip: Barre horizontale (shirorékha) relie les lettres par le haut — script Devanagari

name: Népalais
tip: Même script Devanagari que le hindi — quasiment indistinguable visuellement, contexte géographique décisif

name: Bengali
tip: Barre horizontale comme le Devanagari mais lettres plus rondes et pendantes sous la ligne

name: Dzongkha
tip: Script tibétain — lettres empilées verticalement, aspect très différent du Devanagari

name: Singhalais
tip: Grandes boucles rondes et fermées — encore plus circulaire que le tamoul, lettres très compactes

name: Thaï
tip: Petites spirales sur les lettres, tons marqués au-dessus — plus ornemental et complexe que le laotien

name: Laotien
tip: Similaire au thaï mais lettres plus rondes et moins ornées — moins de diacritiques au-dessus

name: Khmer
tip: Lettres très complexes avec sous-scripts — aspect plus anguleux et dense que le thaï et le laotien

name: Vietnamien
tip: Diacritiques empilés uniques au monde (ắ, ề, ộ) — deux signes superposés sur une même lettre, aucune autre langue ne fait ça

name: Indonésien
tip: Latin standard sans diacritiques — ng, ny fréquents, mots souvent courts

name: Malais
tip: Quasi identique à l'indonésien — ng, ny, kh fréquents, très difficile à distinguer
